comments.text: I have a question concerning a case \((G_1,G_2)=(K_4,K_3)\), from #595. I would appreciate it if anyone has an idea. Let \(A\) and \(F\) be finite graphs, with \(e(F)\ge 2\). Given an \(A\)-free graph \(H\), define \(X_F(H)\) to be the hypergraph whose vertices are the edges of \(H\), and whose hyperedges are the edge-sets of copies of \(F\) in \(H\). Suppose that\[ \chi(X_F(H))>\aleph_0. \]Is there a known way to thin or extend \(H\), while preserving the above, so that the remaining obstruction becomes canonical like so? I want a family of edges\[ \{g_{\theta,\bar i}:\theta\in\Theta,\ \bar i\in [L]^{a(\theta)}\}, \]where \(\Theta\) is countable and \(L\) is uncountable, such that: 1. the restricted copy hypergraph on these edges has chromatic number \(>\aleph_0\); 2. for any finite list of these edges, whether they form the edge-set of a copy of \(F\) depends only on the finitely many labels \(\theta\) and on the order pattern of the tuples \(\bar i\). This is motivated by the example:\[ (A,F)=(K_4,K_3). \]Then \(H\) is \(K_4\)-free, and \(X_{K_3}(H)\) records the triangles of \(H\). So \(\chi(X_{K_3}(H))>\aleph_0\) means that no countable edge-colouring of \(H\) avoids a monochromatic triangle. Is this plausible or known? Any pointers to the literature would also be very helpful.
